branchingScenario.title: AN74.1-4 | Patterns of Inheritance — Clinical Case

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A couple seeks genetic counselling after their first child is diagnosed with cystic fibrosis. Work through this case exploring autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, and X-linked inheritance patterns.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified inheritance patterns and demonstrated strong understanding of genetic counselling principles.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review recurrence risks for autosomal recessive disorders and X-linked inheritance.
Revisit autosomal dominant vs recessive vs X-linked inheritance patterns, carrier status, and anticipation.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A young couple presents for genetic counselling. Their 2-year-old son has been diagnosed with cystic fibrosis (CF), an autosomal recessive disorder. Both parents are unaffected.

Autosomal recessive disorders require two copies of the mutant allele for phenotypic expression. Both parents must be heterozygous carriers. With each pregnancy, there is a 25% chance of having an affected child.

The family history also reveals the father's mother has Huntington's disease — an autosomal dominant disorder with vertical transmission through every generation, where an affected heterozygous parent has a 50% chance of passing the mutant allele to each offspring.

branchingQuestion.question: The couple asks about the risk for their next pregnancy. Both parents are carriers for CF (autosomal recessive). What is the chance their next child will be affected?

alternatives.text: 25% — both parents are heterozygous, so 1 in 4 chance of homozygous affected offspring
50% — because one allele from each parent is mutant

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: 50% is the carrier (heterozygous) probability. For autosomal recessive, both alleles must be mutant: Aa x Aa gives 25% AA, 50% Aa, 25% aa. Only 25% are affected.

alternatives.text: 100% — because they already have one affected child

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Each pregnancy is an independent event. With two carrier parents (Aa x Aa), each child has a 25% chance of being affected (aa), regardless of previous children.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Each pregnancy carries a 25% risk of an affected child.

The father is concerned about Huntington's disease from his mother. You explain that Huntington's shows anticipation — the phenomenon where a genetic disease becomes more severe in successive generations, due to trinucleotide repeat expansion. Myotonic dystrophy is another classic example.

The family also mentions a maternal uncle with haemophilia A. X-linked recessive disorders predominantly affect males because they have only one X chromosome and cannot be carriers in the traditional sense — a single mutant allele on the X causes disease.

branchingQuestion.question: The father wants to know about Huntington's disease. His mother is affected. What is the phenomenon where the disease becomes more severe in successive generations?

alternatives.text: Anticipation — caused by trinucleotide repeat expansion across generations
Penetrance — the gene becomes more penetrant with each generation

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Penetrance describes whether a genotype produces a phenotype. The progressive worsening across generations is called anticipation, caused by expanding trinucleotide repeats.

alternatives.text: Epistasis — other genes amplify the disease effect over generations

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Epistasis is when one gene masks or modifies another gene's effect. The correct term is anticipation — trinucleotide repeat expansions grow larger each generation, causing earlier onset and greater severity.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key genetics principles:

  • Autosomal dominant: 50% transmission risk, vertical pattern, heterozygous expression
  • Autosomal recessive: 25% risk when both parents are carriers (heterozygous), requires two mutant alleles
  • X-linked recessive: Predominantly affects males (single X chromosome)
  • Anticipation: Disease worsens across generations due to trinucleotide repeat expansion (Huntington's, myotonic dystrophy)
